vulnerability.title: Set SSH Daemon LogLevel to VERBOSE
vulnerability.description: The VERBOSE parameter configures the SSH daemon to record login and logout activity. To specify the log level in SSH, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: LogLevel VERBOSE
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" cp "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode

triage_decision.reason: Changing the SSH LogLevel to VERBOSE is a standard hardening practice to improve auditability. The remediation involves adding a configuration file in the sshd_config.d directory, which is a non-destructive way to manage SSH settings without modifying the main config file directly.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for increased log file size/disk usage due to more verbose logging

llm_verdict.message:
The remediation cannot be completed because the current user session is encountering a critical system error: `You are required to change your password immediately (password expired). Password change required but no TTY available.` 

This error is blocking all command execution, including `mkdir`, `id`, and even basic shell commands. Since I do not have a TTY to perform a password change and cannot execute any commands to reset the password or extend its validity, I am unable to proceed with the planned remediation. 

Please resolve the expired password issue for the execution user to allow the remediation to continue.

per_turn.command: echo "The user session is encountering an expired password error which is blocking all command execution via run_cmd. I will attempt to verify if I can bypass this by checking if any command works, but if the system truly requires a password change for the current user, I may be unable to proceed without administrative intervention to reset the password or extend its life."

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan is sound and follows the recommended approach of using the sshd_config.d directory to manage compliance settings. Removing existing LogLevel entries in the main config via sed ensures that the new setting in the drop-in file takes precedence and avoids configuration ambiguity.
concerns: Using 'sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel/d" /etc/ssh/sshd_config"' might remove lines that are commented out if the regex is too broad, although in this case, it targets the active configuration which is the intent.
suggested_improvements: Before restarting the service, it is best practice to run 'sshd -t' to validate the configuration syntax to prevent locking the user out due to a syntax error.
